ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301403 covers soybean whole raw; white variety is a cultivar subset — match is defensible at species level.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical dry serving of raw white soybeans is approximately 30 g, equivalent to about 2 tablespoons of whole dried beans before soaking or cooking.

nutrient_source.title: White soybean, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F09020025 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source. USDA FoodData Central (NDB 16108, Soybeans, mature seeds, raw) was cross-referenced to validate macro plausibility; values are consistent.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Soybeans (Glycine max) GI = 18 (low) is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (18 × 30.16 × 0.30) / 100 = 1.6 (rounded to 1 decimal place). Input GI value of 18 and GL of 5.4 per 100 g are consistent with this source; GL per serving recalculated for 30 g portion.

hypertension.key_factor: Exceptionally low sodium (2 mg/100 g) and very high potassium (1797 mg/100 g) yielding a highly favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio
hypertension.guidance: White soybeans are highly suitable for hypertension management; their negligible sodium content and exceptionally high potassium (1797 mg/100 g) strongly support blood pressure reduction consistent with DASH dietary principles, and their soluble fibre (9.3 g/100 g) and calcium (277 mg/100 g) provide additional vasodilatory benefit — however, they must be consumed cooked, and soy-derived processed products (soy sauce, miso) should be avoided due to high added sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 18), high protein (36.5 g/100 g), and high dietary fibre (9.3 g/100 g) collectively attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: White soybeans are well suited for Type 2 Diabetes management; their GI of 18 is among the lowest of any legume, and the combination of 36.5 g/100 g protein and 9.3 g/100 g fibre slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glycaemia — portion control remains important given 30.2 g/100 g total carbohydrate, and raw soybeans must always be cooked before consumption to neutralise trypsin inhibitors that impair protein digestion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High polyunsaturated fat (11.3 g/100 g including ALA omega-3), low saturated fat (2.9 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and high soluble fibre supporting LDL reduction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: White soybeans are cardioprotective; their favourable fatty acid profile (PUFA 11.3 g, SFA 2.9 g per 100 g), zero dietary cholesterol, and high soluble fibre (9.3 g/100 g) support LDL cholesterol reduction, while soy isoflavones have demonstrated modest endothelial benefits in peer-reviewed trials — they should be consumed cooked as part of a varied plant-based diet, and individuals on anticoagulant therapy should maintain consistent soy intake due to vitamin K content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory isoflavones, omega-3 ALA, and high fibre supporting gut microbiome and immune regulation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: White soybeans may benefit rheumatoid arthritis patients through their isoflavone content (with demonstrated anti-inflammatory properties), ALA omega-3 fatty acids, and high fibre supporting gut microbiome diversity — they must be consumed cooked, and patients on disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs should discuss large soy quantities with their rheumatologist due to potential isoflavone-drug interactions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White soybean, raw (Glycine max) is a mature dried legume seed with pale cream to white round seeds, belonging to the Fabaceae family. It is one of the most nutritionally dense plant foods available, providing 446 kcal per 100 g with an exceptional macronutrient profile: 36.5 g protein, 19.9 g fat (predominantly polyunsaturated), and 30.2 g carbohydrate with 9.3 g dietary fibre. Raw white soybeans must always be cooked before consumption — boiling, steaming, or pressure-cooking — as they contain trypsin inhibitors and lectins that impair protein digestion and can cause gastrointestinal distress when consumed raw. The cooked beans have a mild, slightly nutty flavour with a firm yet creamy texture. Soybeans are a cornerstone ingredient across East and Southeast Asian cuisines, used to produce tofu, tempeh, miso, soy milk, edamame, and soy sauce. Clinically, white soybeans are notable for their very low glycemic index (GI 18), making them one of the most glycaemia-friendly carbohydrate sources available. Their potassium content (1797 mg/100 g) is exceptionally high, supporting blood pressure management, while their zero cholesterol and favourable PUFA-to-SFA ratio support cardiovascular health. Soy isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) have been studied for anti-inflammatory, bone-protective, and modest cardiovascular benefits. White soybeans are suitable for vegan, vegetarian, halal, kosher, and gluten-free diets.
common_user_queries: Are raw soybeans safe to eat?
What is the protein content of soybeans?
What is the GI of soybeans?
Can I eat soybeans if I have high blood pressure?
What are white soybeans used for?
How do I cook raw soybeans?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F09020025, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central NDB 16108 (Soybeans, mature seeds, raw) with consistent macro values. GI value of 18 confirmed in University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Saturated fat, MUFA, PUFA, and micronutrient values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central where HPB did not report them, with source noted.
notes: 1. Core macros (protein_g 36.49, fat_g 19.94, carbohydrate_g 30.16) are all non-zero and well-supported; no zero-macro justification required. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; soybeans are a plant food and contain no dietary cholesterol (naturally zero). Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no B12. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central for raw soybeans. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 2. Saturated_fat_g (2.88), monounsaturated_fat_g (4.40), polyunsaturated_fat_g (11.26), and all micronutrient values not reported in HPB source were supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 16108 (Soybeans, mature seeds, raw), which is an approved secondary source. 3. Raw soybeans contain trypsin inhibitors and lectins; they are not safe for direct consumption without cooking. All health guidance in this record assumes cooked preparation. 4. Soy isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) may interact with thyroid medications, anticoagulants, and certain hormone-sensitive conditions; clinical caution is noted in health_context guidance. 5. GI glycemic load per serving recalculated for 30 g serving: (18 × 30.16 × 0.30) / 100 = 1.6. Input GL of 5.4 appears to be per 100 g basis, not per serving; per-serving GL used in this record. 6. No -1 sentinel values remain unexplained; all -1 fields in the template have been populated from USDA FoodData Central or set to 0.0 where confirmed zero.
